chapter_summary: At Shaunaka's request, Suta (Sauti) narrates in detail the life-prolonging Astika story, beginning with its deep origin. In the devayuga, two beautiful sisters and daughters of Prajapati, Kadru and Vinata, were the wives of the sage Kashyapa. Pleased with them, Kashyapa offered each a boon. Kadru chose a thousand naga (serpent) sons of equal splendor; Vinata chose two sons surpassing Kadru's in strength, energy and valor, and was granted one-and-a-half such sons. After bidding them carefully bear the embryos, Kashyapa departed for the forest. In time Kadru laid a thousand eggs and Vinata two, which the maidservants kept warm in vessels for five hundred years. After five hundred years Kadru's thousand sons emerged, but Vinata's pair did not. Impatient and ashamed, Vinata broke open one of her eggs and found a son whose upper body was formed but lower body unmanifest. Enraged, this son cursed her: because she broke the egg prematurely out of greed, she would become a slave for five hundred years to her rival Kadru, but her other son would later free her from that bondage if she did not maim him too, and instead patiently awaited his full birth. This cursing son becomes Aruna, seen in the sky at every dawn. Later, in due time, Garuda, the slayer of serpents, was born; immediately upon birth he abandoned Vinata and flew into the sky to seek his ordained food, being ravenously hungry.

story.title: The Boons of Kadru and Vinata and the Birth of Aruna and Garuda
story.arc: Two sister-wives of Kashyapa each ask a boon: Kadru chooses a thousand equal serpent sons, Vinata chooses two superior sons. Vinata's impatience leads her to break an egg prematurely, producing the half-formed Aruna, who curses her to five hundred years of slavery to her rival Kadru, while promising that her second son, the mighty Garuda, will one day free her if she lets him be born in his own time. Garuda is then born and at once flies off to find food.
story.timeline_note: Set in the devayuga (age of the gods), a deep-origin backstory framed by Suta's narration to Shaunaka; the eggs are incubated for five hundred years, and Garuda's full birth is awaited beyond a further five hundred years.
story.comic_potential: Sibling-rivalry farce between two co-wives turned into a cosmic bet, plus Vinata's disastrous impatience cracking the egg early and getting cursed by her own indignant half-baked son — a mother scolded by a literally half-finished child.

teachings.teaching: The Astika narrative is called ayushya — life-prolonging — and merely hearing it is held to be auspicious and life-extending.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework, sacred narration is not entertainment but a transmission that lengthens and steadies the listener's prana; attentive shravana (listening) is itself a purifying practice that 'extends life' by aligning awareness with eternal truth.

teachings.teaching: Vinata's greed and impatience, breaking the egg before its time, brings forth an incomplete son and earns her a curse of bondage; her second son will free her only if she waits patiently for his natural maturation.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma this dramatizes that forcing a spiritual result before its appointed ripening deforms it: greed-driven haste (lobha) cripples the fruit and binds the seeker, whereas patient, faithful waiting for the inner birth to complete in its own time yields the liberating power (Garuda) that frees one from bondage.

teachings.teaching: What is chosen with quality (Vinata's two superior sons) ultimately outweighs what is chosen with mere quantity (Kadru's thousand serpents), since one of Vinata's sons becomes the slayer of serpents.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's lens, depth surpasses multiplicity: a single fully-realized inner attainment (the radiant Garuda/Aruna) commands more than a swarm of lesser, scattered forces, mirroring how concentrated one-pointed sadhana overcomes the multitude of restless tendencies.
